title: Chapter 8: First Hint of Cost
content: <!-- Speed: Pivot | D10+D12 -->
Her phone didn't know her face.

She held it at arm's length, the way the setup tutorial had shown her two years ago — twelve to eighteen inches, neutral expression, even lighting. She was in her bathroom, morning light through the frosted window, nothing weird about the conditions.
*Face Not Recognized.*
She turned her head slightly. Tried again.
*Face Not Recognized. Please try again or enter passcode.*
She entered the passcode. The phone opened. She exhaled.
Fine. Glitch. Phones glitched. This was fine.
The text messages started at noon.
She sent one to her mother — a belated reply, three days late, something casual: *Sorry, been busy! I'm fine. Love you.* She watched the send animation. The message zipped away.
No delivery confirmation. No "read" receipt. Just a message, floating in the void, unanswered.
She sent another. To her friend Dani, a coworker from the illustration job she'd stopped showing up to. *Hey. Still alive. Sorry I've been MIA.*
Waited.
The message sat there like a note in a bottle. No read receipt. No typing indicator. After twenty minutes, she checked Dani's social media — Dani was posting, active, online.
The message just hadn't arrived.
Or it had arrived somewhere. Some other void. Some dead-letter dimension where Wren's communications went to stop existing.
She typed a test message to herself. Hit send.
The message appeared. She could see it. But the timestamp was wrong — it said yesterday. Her phone's clock was current. The message had traveled to the wrong time.
A cold thread pulled through her chest.
She went to the bank first.
Not in person — she wasn't ready for that, wasn't ready to stand in front of a teller and be looked through while asking about her own money. She tried the app.

Facial recognition failed immediately. Same message as the phone: *Face Not Recognized.* She entered her password. It worked for a moment — the app loaded, she saw her balance, her recent transactions. Then a popup:
*For your security, please verify your identity using facial recognition.*
She tried again. Failed. The app locked after three attempts.
*Account temporarily restricted. Please visit a branch with valid photo ID.*
She had a driver's license. She'd renewed it eight months ago. The photo showed a woman with anxious posture and a forced smile. It looked like her.
But "looked like her" was becoming a negotiable concept.
She called the bank. The automated system asked for her account number. She read it off her card. Then her Social Security number. Then her mother's maiden name.
*I'm sorry, that information does not match our records.*
"I'm reading it off my card."
*I'm sorry, that information does not match our records. Please hold for a representative.*
She held. Four minutes of hold music — a jazz instrumental that sounded like it was being played by musicians who hated each other.
Then: "Thank you for calling. How can I help you today?"
"I'm locked out of my account. The app's facial recognition isn't working and—"
"Can I get your name, please?"
"Wren. Wren Calloway."
A pause. Keyboard clicks. "I'm not seeing an account under that name."
"It's W-R-E-N. Calloway, C-A-L-L-O-W-A-Y."
More clicks. "No, ma'am. I don't have any record of a Wren Calloway in our system. Are you sure you bank with us?"
She stared at the wall. Her bank card was on the table in front of her. The bank's name was embossed on it. The account number was printed right there.
"I'm sure."
"Would you like me to transfer you to our new accounts department?"
She hung up.
The portfolio was next.

She'd built it over four years. An online gallery — her illustration work, her pen-and-ink drawings, the commissions she'd scraped together from freelance gigs and birthday portraits and one memorable job drawing concept art for a video game that never got funded.
She typed the URL.
*404 — Page Not Found.*

She tried Google. Searched her own name. The results were sparse — a LinkedIn profile that hadn't been updated in two years, a tag in someone else's Instagram post from 2019. No portfolio. No gallery. No evidence that Wren Calloway had ever made anything.
She checked the hosting service. Logged in with her email. The dashboard loaded. Zero sites. Zero domains.
*Welcome! Create your first site today.*
First site.
She'd had a site for four years.
She put on the cashmere coat — the one she'd taken back from Bendel's, because the oversized hoodie felt like a costume from a play that had closed — and walked to Eli's coffee shop.

Eli was the barista. The one from three weeks ago — or was it four? — who'd handed her a latte and looked right past the space where she'd been standing. The first person to not see her. She remembered him: dark curls, a tattoo of a coffee plant on his forearm, a habit of humming while he steamed milk.
The shop was the same. Mismatched furniture, chalk menu board, the smell of burned espresso and old books.
Eli was not there.
A new barista — young, freckled, earnest — took orders behind the counter. Wren stood in line. Well, stood near the line, since nobody made space for her and she'd stopped expecting them to.
When she reached the counter, the barista looked past her to the next customer.
"A large oat milk latte, please," Wren said.
The customer behind her jumped. The freckled barista frowned, scanning the space in front of him.
"Weird," he muttered. "Thought I heard something."
Wren stepped aside. Watched the orders get made. No Eli. No coffee-plant tattoo. No one who might have remembered her, even as a ghost, even as a glitch.
She approached the counter again, closer this time, and picked up a fork from the condiment station. Dropped it on the floor.
CLATTER.
Three heads turned toward the sound. No one turned toward her.
The freckled barista picked up the fork, shrugging. "Must've slipped."
She went home.
Sat on her bed. Looked at the objects of her life — the sketchbook, the pencil cup, the pill bottle, the phone that didn't know her face, the coat that wasn't hers.

None of it was hers. Not anymore. The world was revoking her, piece by piece. Not just the people — the systems. The infrastructure of being a person. The digital scaffolding that said *Wren Calloway exists, she has a bank account, she makes art, she lives at this address, she is real.*
It was all going dark. Like someone pulling plugs from a circuit board, one socket at a time.
She picked up her phone. Typed a message to her mother:
*Something is wrong with me.*
It sent. No delivery receipt. No read receipt. No response.
She typed: *Mom.*
Nothing.
She typed: *I'm here. I'm still here.*
The message sat on her screen, undelivered, a digital artifact with no destination.
The pill bottle sat on the nightstand. Fourteen left. She could hear them rattle when she shifted on the bed — the small, dry sound of dwindling supply.
Her landlord would expect rent on the first. Five days from now. But if her bank account didn't exist, and her name wasn't in the system, and no one could see her—
She lay back on the bed and stared at the ceiling. The water stain. The cracks. The continent nobody would visit.
At least she didn't have to pay rent.
They didn't know she existed.
She laughed. It came out broken, a noise like something cracking in a freezer — slow, structural, irreparable.
She pressed her palms against the mattress. Felt the springs beneath the cheap padding. Felt real. Solid. Present.
But the world didn't agree.
She went to the bathroom mirror.
Standing close, inches away, breathing on the glass. Her breath fogged the surface. She watched it clear.
In the clearing patch, she saw — not herself. The tile wall behind her. The towel rack. The shower curtain with the whales she'd bought on sale three years ago.
She was there. She could feel her feet on the tile, her hands on the sink, her breath in her lungs. But the mirror said otherwise.
She drew a line in the condensation with her finger. Then another. A small shape — a bird, rough and quick, the kind of sketch she made when her hand moved faster than her brain.
It lasted three seconds before the fog evaporated and the glass went clear.
Her drawing disappeared. Her reflection stayed gone.
The phone buzzed. She checked it.
A text from an unknown number. No content. Just a timestamp — three minutes ago.
She hadn't sent it.
She stared at the empty message, at the digits she didn't recognize, at the proof that something, somewhere, still knew she had a phone.
Then she set the phone face-down on the sink and went to bed.
